Author: Frank Schwichtenberg
Title: Part 1: I Built 22K Lines of Production Go in 20 Hours. The Secret Wasn’t the AI.
Text:
Part 1: I Built 22K Lines of Production Go in 20 Hours. The Secret Wasn’t the AI.
The Vibe Coding Hangover
The productivity gains from AI coding were real — until you tried to ship the output. That’s when the process vacuum showed up.
Somewhere around the fourth hour of debugging AI-generated code that “worked” in the demo but collapsed under its first real test, you start to wonder if the productivity gains were ever real.
You’re not alone. Andrej Karpathy coined the term “vibe coding” in February 2025 — “fully give in to the vibes, embrace exponentials, forget that the code even exists.” It was catchy. Collins Dictionary made it Word of the Year.
And then something funny happened: Karpathy went to build his own serious project, Nanochat, and hand-coded it. He tried Claude and Codex agents but found them “net unhelpful.” The guy who named the movement abandoned it when the stakes were real.
The data backs up his instinct. Google’s 2025 DORA report found that 90% of developers now use AI in their workflow — a 14% jump year-over-year. But here’s the number that should make engineering leaders sit up: teams using AI saw 9% more bugs, 91% more code review time, and 154% larger pull requests. AI adoption had a negative relationship with software delivery stability. More AI, worse outcomes.
That’s not a tool problem. That’s a process problem.
I hit this wall myself on a previous project called ai-resume. I was micromanaging the agent like that boss — the one who stands behind your chair and tells you which keys to press — and it sort of worked, until I added mypy to the pipeline.
The agent’s bright idea? Exempt about 40% of the files so the PEP 484 violations would “go away.” It optimized for making the check pass, not for the outcome the check was supposed to ensure. That was the moment it clicked: specifying the tool isn’t enough. You have to specify the objective — all files pass type checking, no exemptions — or the agent will cheerfully dismantle your guardrails to satisfy the letter of your instructions.
The Actual Result
When you fix the process around the model, the output stops looking like AI code and starts looking like engineering.
Let me show you what happens when you fix the process.
Project Kahi is a modern process supervisor for POSIX systems — a container-first replacement for Python’s supervisord, written in Go. It ships as a single static binary with zero runtime dependencies. Here’s what the codebase looks like after roughly 20 hours of development time with Claude Code:
- 22,123 lines of Go across 108 source files
- 111 features defined, 111 features passing
- 63% of the codebase is test code — 13,973 lines of tests against 8,150 lines of production code (86.9% coverage)
- 85% coverage floor, enforced at the CI level
- FIPS 140 compliance via Go 1.26’s native
GOFIPS140
-- one binary, always compliant, no CGO, no separate build - Multi-architecture containers across 6 Linux architectures plus Darwin, published to
ghcr.io
through GoReleaser
That test ratio is the number I want you to sit with. Not the 22K total lines — any sufficiently chatty model can generate volume. The fact that nearly two-thirds of the output is verification code tells you something about how the AI was operating. It wasn’t freelancing. It was proving its own work, feature by feature, because the system demanded proof.
These patterns didn’t start with Kahi. They were extracted from a previous production project that burned me first, then generalized into a reusable scaffold, then validated on Kahi as a greenfield test.
The code is boring. It’s production-grade. It’s indistinguishable from what a senior engineer would write by hand. That’s the point.
Why Prompts Are Necessary But Not Sufficient
Prompt engineering is the lowest layer of the stack. The engineers stuck there are ignoring everything above it.
If you’ve spent the last two years exclusively focused on “prompt engineering,” I have bad news: you’ve been optimizing the lowest layer of the stack and ignoring everything above it.
Mike Mason put it well in January 2026: “Context engineering has displaced prompt engineering as the critical discipline.” The challenge isn’t getting models to write code. It’s ensuring they see the right information, the right constraints, and the right feedback loops at the right time.
Think about it this way. You’d never give a junior developer commit access to main
with no CI, no linting, no code review, and a verbal instruction to "build something good." But that's exactly what most teams do with AI agents. They open a chat, type a prompt, and hope the output is usable.
The prompt still matters — it’s always underneath. But the process vacuum around the model is what’s actually killing your outcomes.
When Amazon launched Kiro in July 2025 — an entire IDE built around spec-driven development with generated requirements, design docs, and task files — it validated what practitioners were already discovering: the unlock isn’t writing a better prompt in isolation. It’s building the system of constraints, feedback loops, and outcome-oriented contracts that the agent operates within — the prompts are still in there, but they’re an implementation detail, not the primary lever.
What Kahi taught me is that the same engineering disciplines you already believe in — CI/CD, code review, coverage enforcement, immutable specifications — are exactly the disciplines that make AI agents trustworthy. Prompts are to agentic engineering what assembly language is to software development — still there, still important, but not where you spend your day. The real skill is applying the engineering disciplines you already know to a new kind of teammate.
Three Layers: Constitution, Contract, Cage
Every AI-assisted project needs three layers of constraint, and they work at very different altitudes.
Here’s the mental model I’ve settled on.
The Constitution
Before the agent writes a single line of code, it reads the rules it can never break.
Persistent, not per-session. In Kahi, the constitution is .specify/memory/constitution.md
-- a document that defines non-negotiable rules for the entire project lifecycle. This is where you encode decisions that should never be debated mid-implementation.
Scope of control. Kahi’s constitution specifies FIPS 140 compliance as a requirement. It bans specific language patterns in code comments and commit messages. It mandates conventional commit format. It sets the coverage floor.
Document-level enforcement. These rules exist at the document level, not the prompt level. They’re not something you retype every session. They’re persistent, version-controlled, and loaded automatically. The agent literally cannot “decide” to skip FIPS compliance because the constitution is injected into every context window before work begins.
If you take one thing from this article, let it be this: write down three things the agent is never allowed to do. Put them in a file. Commit that file to your repo. You just built your first constitution.
The Contract
The constitution sets principles. The contract sets scope — and makes that scope machine-readable.
A data structure, not a document. Kahi’s contract is feature_list.json
-- a machine-readable specification of all 111 features the project must implement. Each feature entry has an id
, category
, title
, description
, a list of testing_steps
, a passes
boolean, and a dependencies
array linking it to prerequisite features.
Immutable by design. The critical rule: feature_list.json
is immutable except for the passes
field. The agent cannot add features, remove features, reword descriptions, or reorder priorities. The only thing it can change is flipping passes
from false
to true
after it implements and verifies a feature. If requirements need to change, that's a human decision -- you go back to the planning phase and re-engage.
No ambiguity, no drift. This is not a prompt. It’s a data structure that CI can validate against a JSON schema. The agent selects the first feature where passes
is false
and all dependencies have passes: true
, implements it, tests it, marks it passing, commits, and moves on. No scope creep. No "I also refactored the logging while I was in there."
Reward signal, not micromanagement. If this sounds familiar, it should. In reinforcement learning, you never tell the agent how to solve a problem. You define a reward signal — a measure of success — and an environment with constraints, then let the agent iterate until it discovers a policy that works.
Silver et al. argued in their 2021 paper “Reward Is Enough” that a sufficiently rich reward signal in a sufficiently rich environment is all you need to produce intelligent behavior.
That’s exactly what’s happening here. The acceptance criteria in feature_list.json
are the reward signal. The constitution is the environment boundary. You don't micromanage the implementation -- you define what success looks like and let the agent find its way there. The same pattern Anthropic uses to train Claude (Constitutional AI uses a short set of natural-language principles as the basis for a reward model) is the same pattern you use to direct it. It's outcome engineering all the way down.
The Cage
The cage makes it physically impossible for bad code to reach your main branch.
CI with teeth. In Kahi, an 85% test coverage threshold is enforced at the CI level — if coverage drops below the floor, the build fails and the PR cannot merge.
Agent-level enforcement. Five Claude Code hooks enforce quality at the agent level: one blocks destructive bash commands, one rejects AI-isms in PR descriptions, one protects secrets files from modification, one auto-formats code after every edit, and one runs go vet
plus the full test suite when the agent tries to end its session.
Testing as steering, not safety net. That last hook is the most important. The agent cannot stop working until the tests pass. Testing isn’t a safety net you deploy after the fact. It’s a steering mechanism that shapes every implementation decision the agent makes. The agent writes tests not because you asked nicely in a prompt — it writes tests because it literally cannot ship without them.
The result: 63% of the codebase is test code at 86.9% coverage, not because anyone told the agent to “write lots of tests,” but because the cage made testing the path of least resistance.
What This Changes About Your Team
You already know how to do this. You just haven’t applied it to your AI agents yet.
If you’re an engineering leader reading this, I want to be direct: you already know how to do this.
Coverage gates? You have those. CI pipelines? Running them already. Code review standards? Documented. Immutable release specs? Probably somewhere in your JIRA workflow, even if nobody loves the process.
The mistake most teams make with AI coding agents is treating them as a fundamentally new problem that requires fundamentally new skills. It doesn’t. It requires applying your existing disciplines — the ones you fought to establish over years of hard-won experience — to a new kind of teammate.
The agent is a junior developer who works at machine speed, never gets tired, and has no ego about rewrites. That’s powerful.
But like any junior developer, it will take every shortcut you leave open. The question isn’t just “how do I write better prompts?” — it’s “what constraints, acceptance criteria, and validation loops would I put on any engineer who writes this fast and this confidently?” The prompt becomes one input among many. The system around it is what determines the outcome.
You already know the answer. You just haven’t applied it yet.
What Comes Next
The engineers getting the best output from AI agents aren’t writing better prompts. They’re building tighter cages.
This is Part 1 of a series. In Part 2, I’ll walk through the specforge workflow that generates constitutions, specifications, and contracts before the agent writes a line of code — the planning phase that most AI-coding articles skip entirely. Part 3 covers the quality gate architecture in detail: hooks, CI enforcement, and the feedback loops that keep the cage locked.
The engineers getting the best output from AI agents aren’t the ones agonizing over the perfect prompt. They’re the ones building tighter cages — and letting the prompts become what they should be: an implementation detail inside a well-engineered system.
